explanations.1: This disables spell-checking for this entire file, which is common in Docker files since they contain technical terms that spell-checkers might flag incorrectly.
explanations.12: This line pulls a pre-made image that already has PHP 8.2 and the FPM (FastCGI Process Manager) installed, so we don't have to install them manually.
explanations.20: We're declaring the ARG variables again here even though we defined them earlier — this is required in Docker because ARG values don't persist across different RUN commands.
explanations.24: We're creating a new user group with a specific numeric ID (gid) that matches the one on the host machine, which helps avoid file permission issues when sharing folders.
explanations.25: We're creating a new user account with home directory, matching the uid/gid from the host machine so files created inside the container are owned by the right user outside.
explanations.30: We're storing a URL to a JSON file that lists all available Chrome versions and their download locations, which we'll query later to find the exact version we want.
explanations.46: This line queries the JSON file to find the download URL for a specific Chrome browser version on Linux, using jq to filter through the data.
explanations.49: The -j flag tells unzip to extract files directly into the destination folder without creating a subfolder, so we get the executables right where we need them.
explanations.55: Similar to line 46, but this time we're finding the download URL for the chromedriver (the tool that controls Chrome) instead of the browser itself.
explanations.60: This makes the chromedriver file executable so it can actually be run, though the variable name here says 'chrome' which might be slightly confusing since it's actually the driver.
explanations.66: This copies the composer tool (a PHP dependency manager) from a separate pre-built image into our image, rather than downloading and compiling it ourselves.
